## CERN

At CERN, we probe the fundamental structure of particles that make up everything around us. We do so using the world's largest and most complex scientific instruments.

# The Intersecting Storage Rings

**The Intersecting Storage Rings is a former particle accelerator at CERN. It was the world's first hadron collider**

On 27 January 1971, two beams of protons collided in the Intersecting Storage Rings (ISR) for the first time.  Physicists had planned this machine in the 1960s, based on the idea that smashing two particle beams head on would give much higher energies than colliding a single beam of particles with a fixed target. It was the world’s first hadron collider, and proved pivotal in changing the views of members of the physics community who doubted the usefulness of hadron colliders. The ISR began operation in 1971 and ran until 1984, holding  the luminosity record for hadron colliders until 2004.

The ISR was composed of two interlaced rings each with a diameter of 300 metres. Each ring contained a beam pipe surrounded by magnets to direct the circulating particles. Protons circulated in opposite directions and collided with a maximum centre-of-mass energy of 62 GeV. This is the equivalent of a 2000 GeV beam hitting a stationary target. The [Proton Synchrotron](/about/accelerators/proton-synchrotron), which is still in operation, fed proton beams into the ISR.

The ISR performed the first-ever proton-proton and proton-antiproton collisions. It was also where stochastic cooling was first developed. This technique reduces both the transverse dimension of the beam and the spread in the energy of the particles. The technique was adopted by the[Super Proton Synchrotron](/about/accelerators/super-proton-synchrotron) for proton-antiproton collisions and is still used in the [Antiproton Decelerator](/about/accelerators/antiproton-decelerator). The ISR paved the way for later accelerators as it hinted at the smaller elements of protons which are now known to be quarks and gluons.

About 15% of the research done at the ISR was directed at improving the machine itself, which grew increasingly sophisticated during its 13 years of operation. The detector expertise gained through operating the ISR  made later projects such as the Large Hadron Collider possible

After the ISR shut down in 1984, CERN’s focus shifted to the planned [Large Electron-Positron Collider](/about/accelerators/large-electron-positron-collider) but the ISR tunnel is still used for storage and magnet work.

The ISR was a particle accelerator at CERN. It was the world's first hadron collider, and ran from 1971 to 1984, with a maximum center of mass energy of 62

## Contents

# Intersecting Storage Rings

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/ISR_Surface.jpg/250px-ISR_Surface.jpg)
![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/Intersecting_Storage_Rings_01.png/330px-Intersecting_Storage_Rings_01.png)

The **ISR** (standing for "**Intersecting Storage Rings**") was a [particle accelerator](/wiki/Particle_accelerator "Particle accelerator") at [CERN](/wiki/CERN "CERN"). It was the world's first [hadron](/wiki/Hadron "Hadron") [collider](/wiki/Collider "Collider"), and ran from 1971 to 1984, with a maximum [center of mass](/wiki/Center_of_mass "Center of mass") [energy](/wiki/Energy "Energy") of 62 [GeV](/wiki/GeV "GeV"). From its initial startup, the collider itself had the capability to produce particles like the [J/ψ](/wiki/J/%CF%88_particle "J/ψ particle") and the [upsilon](/wiki/Upsilon_particle "Upsilon particle"), as well as observable [jet structure](/wiki/Particle_jet "Particle jet"); however, the [particle detector](/wiki/Particle_detector "Particle detector") experiments were not configured to observe events with large [momentum](/wiki/Momentum "Momentum") [transverse](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/transverse "wikt:transverse") to the [beamline](/wiki/Beamline "Beamline"), leaving these discoveries to be made at other experiments in the mid-1970s. Nevertheless, the construction of the ISR involved many advances in [accelerator physics](/wiki/Accelerator_physics "Accelerator physics"), including the first use of [stochastic cooling](/wiki/Stochastic_cooling "Stochastic cooling"), and it held the record for [luminosity](/wiki/Luminosity_(scattering_theory) "Luminosity (scattering theory)") at a hadron collider until surpassed by the [Tevatron](/wiki/Tevatron "Tevatron") in 2004.[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:2-2)

## History

The ISR was proposed in 1964 for conducting the head-on proton-proton collisions at a beam energy of 28 GeV; to the study of the new particles created in such collisions.  The project was approved within a year.

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/ISR33.jpg/330px-ISR33.jpg)

The idea of colliding beams was first conceived by a group at [Midwestern Universities Research Association](/wiki/Midwestern_Universities_Research_Association "Midwestern Universities Research Association") (MURA) in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), as a way to have collisions at an increased [center of mass energy](/wiki/Center_of_mass "Center of mass"). The MURA group also invented [radiofrequency](/wiki/Radio_frequency "Radio frequency") (RF) stacking technique to accumulate the proton beams of sufficient intensity.[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1) CERN Council looked into this novel idea in 1957, and an Accelerator Research (AR) group was established to look into the possibilities of having such a facility. AR studied the two-way [fixed-field alternating gradient](/wiki/Fixed-field_alternating_gradient_accelerator "Fixed-field alternating gradient accelerator") (FFAG) accelerator for [plasma acceleration](/wiki/Plasma_acceleration "Plasma acceleration") and for an electron collider. In 1960 when the construction of the [Proton Synchrotron](/wiki/Proton_Synchrotron "Proton Synchrotron") was completed, the AR group focussed on a proton-proton collider. To check the feasibility and yield of the RF stacking method, the CERN Electron Storage and Accumulation Ring (CESAR) on a smaller scale compared to ISR, was proposed in 1960 and successfully tested by 1964; followed by the official proposal of ISR in the same year, when the AR group presented the technical design report.[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:2-2)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

In 1971 itself, detectors were set up by 12 experimental teams at five intersecting points of ISR.[[5]](#cite_note-:0-5)

The combination of the [CERN Proton Synchrotron](/wiki/CERN_Proton_Synchrotron "CERN Proton Synchrotron") (CPS) and ISR also enabled the study of collisions using particles other than the proton, such as the [deuteron](/wiki/Deuteron "Deuteron"), [alpha particles](/wiki/Alpha_particle "Alpha particle"), and [antiprotons](/wiki/Antiproton "Antiproton"). [[6]](#cite_note-6)

The initial goal and motivation for ISR was the following.

During the end stages of ISR, the beam energies were hiked up to a maximum value of 31.4 GeV.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## Accelerator

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/ISR11.jpg/330px-ISR11.jpg)

The accelerator was made up of two magnetic rings (located in France), each with a circumference of 942m. The rings interlaced together such that they would meet at eight intersecting regions for colliding proton beams. The 28 GeV proton beams would come from the CERN Proton Synchrotron (CPS) located about 200 meters away (In Switzerland)[[5]](#cite_note-:0-5)

### Major leaps in accelerator technology

#### RF stacking

CERN Electron Storage and Accumulation Ring (CESAR) and later ISR were among the first colliders to utilize the RF beam stacking method to increase intensity. In the previous years, the construction of hadron colliders was avoided as it appeared to be unfruitful due to the unavailability of any stacking method. Since ISR, every other collider has utilized the RF stacking method.[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1)

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/ISR22.jpg/330px-ISR22.jpg)

#### Schottky noise and stochastic beam cooling

[Schottky noise](/wiki/Shot_noise "Shot noise") is a signal generated by a finite number of randomly distributed particles in a beam. In 1972 Wolfgang Schnell found the longitudinal and transverse Schottky noise signals at ISR. This made it evident that [stochastic](/wiki/Stochastic "Stochastic") beam damping was possible. And brought up a new window for non-invasive beam diagnostics and the need to have an active cooling system for reducing the size and momentum spread of the beam.[[10]](#cite_note-:3-10)
The Schottky signals gave a precise description of how the density of the beam stack varied with betatron frequency. After demonstrating the damping of [betatron oscillations](/wiki/Betatron_oscillations "Betatron oscillations"), [stochastic cooling](/wiki/Stochastic_cooling "Stochastic cooling") of the antiprotons beams was widely used to enhance [luminosities](/wiki/Luminosity_(scattering_theory) "Luminosity (scattering theory)") in proton-antiproton collisions. After ISR, the [proton-antiproton collider](/wiki/Super_Proton%E2%80%93Antiproton_Synchrotron "Super Proton–Antiproton Synchrotron") at [Super Proton Synchrotron](/wiki/Super_Proton_Synchrotron "Super Proton Synchrotron") employed the same technique for increasing luminosities, so did the other colliders such as [Tevatron](/wiki/Tevatron "Tevatron").[[10]](#cite_note-:3-10)

#### Thin-walled vacuum chambers

ISR group designed and set-up very large thin-walled [vacuum chambers](/wiki/Vacuum_chamber "Vacuum chamber") at the intersecting points where detectors were set up. These were made up of [tin](/wiki/Tin "Tin") and [titanium](/wiki/Titanium "Titanium") and inspired the future vacuum chambers.[[10]](#cite_note-:3-10)

#### Detector magnets

The superconducting solenoid installed at Intersection-1, the Open Axial Field Magnet installed at Intersection-8 and an Air-cored Toroid at Intersection-6, were state-of-the-art magnetic detector systems developed by ISR teams. Almost all collider detectors are now based on larger and improved versions of basic principles of [magnetic detectors](/wiki/Magnetic_detector "Magnetic detector") put forth by ISR.[[10]](#cite_note-:3-10)

enriched_points.expanded_content: Operational from 1971 to 1984, ISR was the first hadron collider, achieving 62 GeV collision energy and pioneering techniques like stochastic cooling.
The J/ψ particle was discovered at the ISR, confirming the existence of charm quarks and reshaping our understanding of particle physics.
Technological innovations from ISR, including stochastic cooling and RF stacking, set the stage for the construction of the Large Hadron Collider (LHC).
